speaker: interviewer
text: Those days that-- in 1938 that preceded your leaving, I wanted to ask you first of all, amongst the non-Jewish friends and associates that you and your parents had, did those-- did those relationships change?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- they faded away. Number one, we no longer lived in the neighborhood where they were. Now we lived in a Jewish neighborhood. And number two, as times got worse, they sort of faded away in just-- it was a natural procession that we hardly saw them anymore. And-- and business got worse all the time at this point already, too. And we just knew that something drastic had to be done, because then came Crystal Night on November the 9th. And in our second apartment-- the first apartment that we lived in that I told you about, the synagogue Bornplatz, we walked quite a ways. The second apartment, we could see the synagogue from the bedrooms of our apartment. And I distinctly remember Crystal Night, when they smashed the synagogue. I saw it burning. I saw it smashed. And we were downtown. And we saw the huge department stores. It's sort of impossible to relate to you, because it would be like Marshall Field's downtown being smashed into 10,000 pieces. And probably most of the big department stores in Hamburg were Jewish owners, so it was catastrophic. It was catastrophic. And that's really when we knew that there's not one minute left now. We just have to get out of here. And there were a few countries that you possibly could go to in South America, Chile and Argentina and Uruguay and so on and so forth. But you still had to have connections. You still had to make it possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So our only connection was Uruguay. And that's when we went to the harbor that night of December the 16th with my four grandparents in this bitter, bitter cold night. And only now that I have my own children, and hopefully grandchildren one day, do I know what that must have been. We, my brother and I-- I was 12, he was 9-- so then we were together with our parents. And as I said, my grandmother still ran downtown with me and got me pretty little dresses. So we were going on this adventure and didn't have the full impact of the whole thing. But of course, my grandparents really never survived that, because they were in the 70s. And they were strong, good-looking, vital people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father's parents died six months after we left, six, seven months after we left. They both caught pneumonia that horrible night in December and got very sick. But then I always felt they really died of a broken heart. The pneumonia was true. They did catch a pneumonia. But they also had no will to live. And so I remember my father sitting shiva. We were just in-- barely in Montevideo. We were still renting. We were still not having our own place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my grandfather, my father's father, ultimately got sick. He was very close with my mother, so I'm convinced that he as well died of a broken heart. I mean there was nothing-- people always say, did they die of a natural death? Maybe they did, but nobody could ever diagnose that the heart was broken.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when my mother went berserk, when her father died. And that's when we had to make it possible to get her out to Montevideo. And that was not easy, because my father--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother, my mother's mother, right. My father, it was hard for him. He didn't know. All he knew was import-export. And he knew the language because of his business. It was very hard. He did all kinds of things. He really was not the kind of person who could have just done anything. It just was-- couldn't do just anything. And so my mother was the one that really helped out there. She was in the first World War a volunteer for the Red Cross, so she had a little bit of an idea how to deal with sick people. So what she did in Montevideo to-- to earn some money, she became a practical nurse. When people would come home from the hospital, she would take care of them. And that's really-- that's really what maintained us to an extent. We did have some money in Switzerland that we brought to Montevideo. But not-- being totally strange, not knowing anything, we put that money into a Jewish bank. It was a logical thing. And that bank went bankrupt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were really, really penniless, even though my brother Kurt always said, how come we never felt poor? I guess we always had a nice apartment. We had our furniture. And then, of course, later on, my grandmother came. But it was a very difficult time. There's no two ways about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Montevideo was a lovely city, a beautiful city, a beach city. And we had-- we went to school right away. We had to learn Spanish. And it was a big problem, because as I said before, my father was very religious. And there were certain amount of opportunities to get a job, but he would never want to work on the Shabbos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember in Montevideo, where we lived then, there was a big patio in the middle. And around it were the rooms, typical South American houses. And I remember him wandering around and around and around during the night. He just said if he works on the Sabbath, then he's giving up what he believes in. And when he does that, then there's nothing left. So he did not take that job.

speaker: interviewee
text: And ultimately, two, three years along the road, there was an opportunity. A cousin of his from Argentina started a wool business in Montevideo and asked my father to join him. And then my father became successful again. But it was a long road. And it was difficult. But we as children really never had a bad time, because we went to school, went to public school there. I had to start from first grade, even though I was in eighth grade, because I didn't know the language. But I made up in my first year for all those eight years, and then went-- consequently went to high school and college in Montevideo. But then to come back to what we started to talk about, my grandmother being the only one left in Germany, we had to get her out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back for a moment to getting your exit papers. Your mother went to the head of the SS and somehow convinced him to give her the proper papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your brother were waiting. After you left the SS headquarters, you went to the hospital, got your father out.

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] Exactly. [GERMAN] Now I'm saying in German. Exactly what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got our clothes together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went home?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got our-- the cabin. I mean, that was where-- last minute, we got a cabin on this boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how you managed to get the passage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only because my parents were travelers with Hamburg America line and knew management and somehow accomplished it that we got a couple of cabins, one for the children and one for my parents. And this was all done in one day. The paper from the Gestapo, getting my father out of the hospital, packing our suitcases, getting some more clothes, and getting to-- the ship that we left on was already-- had already left the harbor, had already left the harbor because it was late at night. On a tender, we were brought onto that ship. And if you look later on, if you look through my documents, you will see that our name is not on the passenger list, because it isn't. We weren't supposed to be on there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I always thought we left with the last boat on an-- in an orderly fashion. I am since told that there were other boats that left. But to the best of my knowledge, from Hamburg I think we were the last boat that left in an orderly fashion. Do you remember the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. December 16th, 1938. I'll never forget that date.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to say goodbye to your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unthinkable. Unthinkable, because we really knew we wouldn't see them again. We just knew it. We didn't even think that we ever would get anybody out. But then, of course, was-- my grandmother did get out. We did get her out. But we never did see my grandfather, my mother's father, or my father's parents back ever again. And my father was particularly close to his mother, so it was-- it was horrendous. It really-- that-- that can't be expressed in any words. I mean, that was like tearing out a heart. That just can't be expressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the voyage like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The voyage was fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in December?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even in December, because we were going into warmer climates. We were going to South America. And then, of course, there were people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many, many Jewish people on that boat that were also heading for Montevideo, Uruguay. And they were from all over Germany. And we became friends with them. And there were-- it was a luxury boat and we went first class. And so that was really fun. That-- we had no idea what the reality of-- as being children, we had no idea what the reality of this whole thing was. We were, I think, on the boat over 2 and 1/2 weeks, so it was a long time. And we played ping pong. And we got to know other people. And I had my pretty dress on that my grandmother had still just bought me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that part was nice. That part was nice. And of course, how-- how it was for my parents, I have no idea. I'm sure it was not half as nice for them, because they knew that they were going to some place that-- I remember when we got there, my brother Kurt looked at my father and says, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? Pappy, what are we going to do here? My father said, I have no idea. We had no idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, I don't think, picked up by anybody or anything. We were just there together with all those other people. And somebody knew some kind of a hotel or a place where we could stay for a few nights. And that's what we did. And there was very little money, because the money from Switzerland hadn't arrived yet. And we didn't physically have it yet. So it was-- money was very tight. And then we rented a couple of rooms with some Jewish people in Montevideo. And I remember they had these funny beds. They were just sort of close together and opened up and there were just-- just draperies in between where my brother and I slept and the people that rented it to us. They-- I guess they were also from Germany. Oh, I don't know really. I can't remember exactly. But they were poor people that-- that rented rooms to-- to earn some money. And it was very primitive, very, very primitive. My mother had to cook on a Primus it was called, some kind of a concoction of whatever it was. And she stood there. And you know, she wasn't used to it. It was so hard for her. And it was difficult times for my parents, very difficult times. The only thing was that ultimately we did rent an apartment. And then our lift-- all our furniture was still in Germany. But since my grandparents were still there, they were able to pack it for us and send it to us to Montevideo, which was very fortunate, so that we had our furniture and pictures and things. My-- my grandmother saw to it that it was all packed. We got it there. I still remember our-- the huge piano that was lifted up to get through the window up there on the second floor. And it didn't get in. I mean, it crashed into 10,000 pieces. We never again had a piano in our lives. [LAUGHS] And it was-- it was difficult. We then rented rooms to other German Jewish people and made money that way. And it was a struggle. It was a struggle probably for two or three years. And then we already got into a nicer apartment. But first we had to get my grandmother out yet. That was the big thing, because my mother really went berserk. I mean, she went nuts when-- when my grandmother was there. And we didn't have any money at all then.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I remember distinctly that she said to me, now we have-- Omi has to come. We have to do it. And she took me-- by this time I'm 13 and six months spoke the language. Language is one-- is my forte. I got that fast, not my mother, but because we were little, we caught it fast. And we went on the streetcar. My mother went to the market and they bought a big bouquet of flowers.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got directions to get to the palace. Palace sounds fancy. It was a small palace. Uruguay was a smaller country. My mother said, you have to go inside this palace. And you have to see that you get to speak to the wife of the president of Uruguay. And there were the two soldiers there. And she said, I know it's impossible, but you have to make it possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I marched with my pretty little dress and the flowers in my arms to the door of the palace and negotiated, talked to the soldiers, cried. I don't know what I did. He did let me in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did get to see the wife of the president of Uruguay. I did tell her my story. She did make one phone call. And on that one phone call, we builded and builded and builded, and ultimately were able to get my grandmother out. We got her papers. We had to send some money for her to get out. And about nine or 10 months later after us, she came. And once she came, we were able to get a better apartment. And she then cooked and baked for us, and cooked and baked for the people that we rented the rooms to. And my father still did this and that and my mother did what I told you what-- ill people. And those years-- but for us, it was, again, not so terrible, because we had a house. We had my grandmother. And we had my mother and father. And I remember I had-- took some dancing lessons and went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for us, it really-- we were very lucky. We were very lucky that we got out on that last boat, because anything after, you know the story. Who were your friends at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montevideo? Well, you see, a lot of people from all over Germany ended up in Montevideo, so we started immediately-- my father, who started [GERMAN], a German-Jewish synagogue. And my mother started the chapter of WIZO, Women's International Zionist Organization. And we were friends with all the people that came from other places from Germany. And only after a while did we-- through school and through other ways, did we become friendly with people who were really born in Uruguay. But it took quite a while. The German-Jewish community, there were many Jewish communities in Montevideo. There was the German Jewish community, the Polish Jewish community, the Sephardic Jewish community. And each one had their own synagogue. In our synagogue, they only spoke German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Uruguayans were very good about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. The Uruguayans were very good to us. My parents loved living in Montevideo. The Uruguayans were very good to us. There was-- the anti-Semitism that people hear about existed in Argentina, but did not exist in Uruguay-- did not exist in Uruguay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I would have to say that my teenage years then in Montevideo were nice. I had-- I worked for-- after high school I was an office help to a neurologist. And I started working [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the operating room of-- he saw to it. He helped me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also from Germany. But he had arrived in Uruguay much sooner. He left-- there were some people who were smart, who left in-- left in '34, '35. And by the time we then came in '38, they were already settled, so they already could help us. And so that was my first job. And then I ultimately became a medical technologist. That was ultimately my profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the war years, did you know what was going?

speaker: interviewee
text: We certainly did. And [INAUDIBLE] that big boat was-- ended up in Uruguay. And we certainly knew about the war years. And we helped where we could and how we could. And-- but it didn't really affect us directly. We just heard about it, of course, through the radio and all over, and did what we could. But it wasn't something that touched us personally. Now, all the aunts and uncles were all already in concentration camps and they were all killed. I mean, my grandfather, my father's father, was one of 11 children. And all those aunts and uncles and cousins, none of them got out. Just my brother's father got out. He-- he had somebody in New York that gave him an affidavit, so he got out with his son. And my father's sister and her husband and the two children went to France. And of course, the war was in France. And my-- my aunt's husband and her son were killed. But the daughter and my aunt stayed in France and ultimately lived in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like when your grandmother came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it was wonderful. Then she brought so much love and so-- and something that we-- it was such a miracle that she could come. And she-- she made our house a home again. And I was very, very close to her. And ultimately when I got married brought my children down so she could still meet them. And she had her friends, you know, all the other elderly ladies. And they went and had coffee in the afternoon. And she had a nice life. And she made our life wonderful. I imagine now that I am a grown person, must have not always been so easy for my father to have a mother-in-law close by. But it always was a wonderful, loving household. And again in Montevideo, I have friends now that always remember no matter how little we had, we always had a lovely Friday night. And everybody always came and visited. And I have friends now that remember that, that are now in the United States. And really, little by little, a nice lifestyle developed in Montevideo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents, in spite of everything, losing their business, losing their money, coming to a strange country, established themselves very nicely.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they were so deeply religious that that helped them a great deal. And touch wood, they were healthy. And they were smart. And they loved each other. And they loved us, so we were a family unit. And we really lived through it [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] happily. Now, I can only speak for myself. For my parents, it was much less happily, because they lost their parents. Their parents died in such a short while after we left, so that was horrendous. But then they would be in touch-- my mother's brother was in Ecuador, so they were in touch with each other. And as I said, my father's brother was in New York, so they were in touch with each other. So life did then go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you lose contact with the family in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The minute we left. The minute we left, everything went up in flames. And, you know, my grandparents died and my grandmother came out. And the rest, I think most of them died in concentration camps. I really don't know exactly all of them. But I just know that there are cousins-- my father's cousins, are in Israel. They left early. They left in '33 or '34. And they live in Israel. And there is another cousin that lives in South Africa. And as I said, my-- my aunt and my cousin lived in Manchester. And my uncle, Poppy's brother, lived in New York. But the rest didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you and your brother went to school.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to school. And we went to synagogue with our parents on Shabbos morning. And we both ended up getting a good education. You know, schools are free. Public schools were free. And I don't know. I got some grants or something. Somehow or other, they paid-- I went to college, but it wasn't like-- you weren't sent away to college. College was in the city of Montevideo, Universidad de Montevideo. And so I slept at home, lived at home, but went to school. So somehow, it was-- they were able to give us an education, both of us. So that was very fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father got back on his feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because my-- his cousin in Argentina, who was in the wool and meat business in Argentina, opened an office in Montevideo and made my father a partner. And then after that, he became successful. And then he did get some restitutions from Germany, my parents did. And so they were comfortable. They had a nice, comfortable lifestyle in Montevideo again. They started traveling again, and so on and so forth. And then when I finished college, I was 21, then I wanted to leave Montevideo. I liked it a lot and I had a happy life there, but it wasn't where I wanted to end up my life, because it was too-- too restricted, too small. It didn't-- I didn't feel I had the opportunities that I was looking for.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at age 21, I announced I would love to go to the United States. They thought I really lost my marbles [LAUGHS] and definitely were not for it. But I have an uncle-- had an uncle here, my mother's cousin, who she was very close to. And I wrote that I would like to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: And really, I myself was a little shocked, because he sent me an affidavit right away. He had become somewhat successful, had-- had a department store in a small town here in Benton, Illinois. And he sent me my affidavit. And at age 21, I came to the United States. My parents didn't give me any money. He said-- they said if you want to go, then go. They paid my-- for my tickets. And they gave me $1,000. And my grandmother quietly gave me a little more. And-- But they really did never think that I would stay and make it here. They thought I would go as far as Rio de Janeiro, where they had had friends, I'd buy a couple of dresses and I'd come back. But I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to pick the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was what I wanted. I can't tell you how come. That's where I wanted to go. And I knew exactly what I wanted to do. I wanted to become a medical technologist. And I had heard about Michael Reese through my aunt and uncle who's-- my aunt's brother was Professor Oppenheimer, was at Michael Reese Hospital. And that's how-- that's what I wanted to do. And the only problem was when I came to the United States, my college degree from Montevideo was not recognized. They didn't-- that was not what they could use here. So I had to go back to night school. In order to become a medical technologist, you had to have two years of college, so alongside my training at Michael Reese, I went to night school to the University of Chicago. Actually, I had finished my 18 months course as a medical technologist prior to really finishing college. I had six more months to go. And so that's how that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the south side of Chicago. I lived at 67th and Kimbark. A German-Jewish family was renting rooms. And I rented a room there. And-- but then that room-- their son came back from the Army and I had to move out. I moved downstairs to a lovely Jewish family whose three daughters had just gotten married. And they were lonely. And so for $8 a week, I-- [LAUGHS] I had breakfast and I had dinner. And it was really very, very lucky, very lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had your first job where?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Michael Reese Hospital. No. My very first job before I went to Michael Reese, I sold hats in a little shop at the Palmer House. [LAUGHS] That was my very first job. But I wanted to-- I wanted to really get to Michael Reese. And-- and I accomplished it that I could take my training there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you already knew English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, you asked my very first job. And now you ask me, did I know English? I knew very, very little. And my very first job was really at Rockaway Beach, Long Island, as a baby nurse. And since I didn't have to talk to the baby, I learned English as I went along there. And I did that for about six weeks or eight weeks, for the summer. And I left Montevideo in March, so I spent April and May with my relatives. And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can't even remember. No. Before I even arrived at my relatives, I was in New York visiting my uncle. That's what it was, my father's brother. And I loved New York so much, so that's why I got the job as a baby nurse, so that-- because my money had run out too. So that's how I started really my career in the United States, in Rockaway Beach.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll break now. And then we'll pick up the story in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
